rationale: trabajo → presente: hace + time expression + que + present tense describes an action that started in the past and is still ongoing — 'I have been working here for three years.'
he sentido → pretérito perfecto compuesto (present perfect): a recently completed experience with relevance to the present moment — the feeling has built up and still matters now.
tiene → presente: states a current general truth or condition, not a past event.

parts: un trabajo tan estable, y que
rationale: han dicho → pretérito perfecto compuesto: colleagues have said this on various occasions up to now — a repeated past action with present relevance.
han tenido → pretérito perfecto compuesto: their claim about never having had such a stable job spans up to the present, linking past experience to the current argument.
haría → condicional: expresses what I would do — a hypothetical outcome contingent on staying, not a certain fact.
fuera → subjuntivo imperfecto: si + imperfect subjunctive opens a contrary-to-fact conditional — I am not braver, so this is hypothetical.

habría fundado → condicional perfecto: the consequence of a contrary-to-fact past condition — what would have already happened by now if things had been different. Together, fuera + habría fundado form the classic si-clause perfect conditional structure.
cumpla → subjuntivo presente: para cuando + a future reference always requires the present subjunctive in Spanish — the turning-40 event is anticipated but not yet real.
habré ahorrado → futuro perfecto: expresses an action that will have been completed by a specific future point. The saving happens before the milestone of turning 40.
hablaré → futuro simple: el año que viene signals a definite future intention — a planned action.
elaboraré → futuro simple: a second planned future action linked to the first, expressing firm intent.

parts: que tomé la decisión correcta porque siempre
rationale: sabré → futuro simple: expresses certainty about a future state of knowing — what I will know when that moment arrives.

he creído → pretérito perfecto compuesto: siempre he creído describes a belief that began in the past and extends continuously up to the present moment, reinforcing that the conviction is still alive now.
